completeness_checks.note: All 2,945 DCWF KSAT elements (1,277 tasks, 1,668 KSAs) evaluated. No exclusions — all workforce elements evaluated including cybersecurity, IT, intelligence, cyber effects, enablers, software engineering, and Data/AI.

rationale_documentation.note: Every rationale file validated as well-formed JSON with complete method coverage. All 2,935 scored files contain STS primary + 4 secondary methods (BERTScore, NLI, Bi-Encoder, Jaccard). All 10 no-relationship files have methodology field documenting the exclusion rationale.

relationship_type_consistency.subset_of_note: No 'Subset of' relationships assigned. Expected: DCWF elements are federal workforce competencies; WIDAI KSAs are domain-specific data/AI competencies. The absence of Subset relationships is consistent with WIDAI being the more specialized framework for data/AI roles, while DCWF elements are always equal to or broader than individual WIDAI KSAs.
relationship_type_consistency.strength_mapping_consistency_check: PASS — Equal relationships have highest mean strength (6.17), followed by Superset (5.51), then Intersects (4.32). This ordering is logically consistent and replicates the pattern observed in STRM-001-ONET and STRM-002-NICE: Equal pairs score highest, Superset pairs score higher than Intersects because the WIDAI KSA is fully contained within the DCWF element.

strength_scoring.scoring_methodology: Cross-Encoder STS (stsb-roberta-base) as primary, raw 0-1 mapped to 0-10 integer

mapping_strength_rationale_consistency.note: All strength values in strm_mapping.json match corresponding rationale files.

comparison_to_prior_strms.interpretation: STRM-003 mean (4.79) falls between STRM-001 (4.18, general-purpose O*NET) and STRM-002 (5.12, cybersecurity-specific NICE). Expected: DCWF shares significant vocabulary with WIDAI via its dedicated Data/AI workforce element (unlike NICE's pure cybersecurity focus), but also contains substantial cybersecurity, intelligence, and cyber effects elements that create vocabulary divergence (unlike O*NET's broad general-purpose language). The intermediate position is analytically coherent.

elements_detail.description: Install or replace network hubs, routers, and switches
elements_detail.assessment: Correctly excluded — physical network hardware installation has no WIDAI relevance.

elements_detail.description: Perform static analysis to mount an image of a drive
elements_detail.assessment: Correctly excluded — disk forensic imaging is a cybersecurity investigation technique.
Correctly excluded — intelligence network analysis, not data/AI competency.

elements_detail.description: Ability to capture a memory image from a host workstation
elements_detail.assessment: Correctly excluded — forensic memory acquisition technique.

elements_detail.description: Ability to conduct disk forensics on multiple images
Ability to extract credentials from hosts
Ability to identify wireless encryption (WEP, WPA)
elements_detail.assessment: Correctly excluded — wireless network security technique.

elements_detail.description: Ability to organize order of the volatility when capturing artifacts
elements_detail.assessment: Correctly excluded — forensic evidence acquisition procedure.

no_relationship_validation.note: All 10 no-relationship classifications are unambiguous. Unlike STRM-002-NICE (which had 428 no-relationship elements with 4 borderline cases), DCWF's no-relationship set is extremely small (0.3%) and consists entirely of tactical military/forensic operations with zero data/AI relevance. No borderline cases identified.

cross_framework_corroboration.note: DCWF gap analysis is pending — gap signals will be derived from weak-strength mapping analysis (thematic clusters in elements scoring ≤3) and cross-referenced against STRM-001-ONET and STRM-002-NICE gap registers. The extremely low no-relationship rate (0.3%) means gap signals will emerge from weak-strength patterns rather than no-relationship boundary analysis.

gap_analysis.status: PASS — gap register pending as separate deliverable

overall_assessment.summary: STRM-003-DCWF completed with full coverage of 2,945 DCWF KSAT elements. Multi-method scoring pipeline (identical to STRM-001-ONET and STRM-002-NICE: STS primary + 4 secondary) processed 2,935 scored pairs and generated 2,945 rationale files with content-specific significance — each referencing actual shared vocabulary, unique terms, and concept-level analysis. All validation checks pass. Relationship type distribution is logically consistent with the strongest strength ordering yet observed (Equal 6.17 > Superset 5.51 > Intersects 4.32). The extremely low no-relationship rate (0.3% vs. 18.3% O*NET, 19.9% NICE) confirms DCWF's significant domain overlap with WIDAI via the Data/AI workforce element. Mean scored strength (4.79) sits between STRM-001 and STRM-002, reflecting DCWF's mixed composition of data/AI-relevant and cybersecurity-specific elements.
key_findings: 99.7% of DCWF elements have WIDAI relationships — the highest match rate of any STRM to date. Confirms DCWF's Data/AI workforce element creates substantial direct overlap with WIDAI.
Only 10 elements (0.3%) classified as 'No relationship' — all unambiguous tactical military/forensic operations. Zero borderline cases.
Strength ordering (Equal > Superset > Intersects) replicates across all three STRMs, confirming multi-method pipeline produces ordinally valid results across diverse frameworks.
Mean scored strength (4.79) is analytically coherent between O*NET (4.18, general-purpose) and NICE (5.12, cybersecurity-specific), reflecting DCWF's mixed domain composition.
WIDAI domain coverage from DCWF is led by TF (463) and SP (443), confirming the technical foundations and security/privacy overlap between federal cyber and data/AI workforce competencies.
By element type: Tasks (mean 4.69) score lower than KSAs (mean 4.86), consistent with tasks being more action-specific and domain-contextualized.
